nodes.body: The frontend Sonar job runs on a fresh runner and never generates or downloads the coverage produced by the separate `frontend` job, so this LCOV path will not exist during analysis. As a result Sonar will report no frontend coverage (or warn about the missing report) even when the tests passed in the previous job.

When Sonar is skipped because the token or organization variable is missing, the Sonar job still completes successfully because only an echo step ran. These `needs.sonar-* .result` values will therefore be `success`, so the Telegram summary reports SonarQube phases as passed instead of skipped and can mislead maintainers about whether analysis actually ran.

The job allows SonarCloud to run when `SONAR_ORGANIZATION` is set, but the scanner args never pass that value as `sonar.organization`. Unlike the backend job, setting a `SONAR_ORGANIZATION` environment variable alone is not a scanner property, so SonarCloud analysis can still fail with a missing organization.

The job gates execution on `SONAR_ORGANIZATION` for SonarCloud, but the scan args never provide `-Dsonar.organization`. Since the scanner action does not derive that property from this custom environment variable, SonarCloud blockchain analysis can fail even when the required variable is configured.

Including the Hardhat `test` directory in `sonar.sources` makes test files count as production source files in SonarQube. That can inflate lines-of-code and quality-gate issues for blockchain code; the tests should be assigned to the test scope instead of the source scope.

This install step is redundant because the following `mvn -f backend/pom.xml verify` runs the full reactor, including both common modules, and reactor dependency resolution does not require pre-installing them into the local Maven repository. Keeping both steps makes CI slower and adds an extra clean/build cycle without changing the verification result.

This job-level condition references the `secrets` context directly. GitHub Actions does not allow secrets in `jobs.<job_id>.if`, so the workflow can fail validation instead of cleanly skipping Sonar when the token is absent; move the secret check into a step-level condition or expose it via an environment variable checked inside the job.

This job-level condition references `secrets.SONAR_TOKEN` directly. The `secrets` context is not available in `jobs.<job_id>.if`, so this can make the workflow invalid rather than skipping the frontend Sonar job when the token is not configured.

This job-level `if` also uses `secrets.SONAR_TOKEN`, which is not permitted in job-level conditions. As written, the blockchain Sonar job can prevent the entire workflow from being accepted instead of being skipped when Sonar secrets are missing.

The generated Dependabot commit prefix does not follow the repository's documented commit-message convention (`[#XX] short message` in CONTRIBUTING.md:212-215). If Dependabot PRs are expected to be merged with their generated commits, this configuration will create commits that deviate from that convention.

This job is gated only on Sonar variables, not on `SONAR_TOKEN`. If the host/organization variables are configured but the token secret is missing or unavailable (for example on forked pull requests), the Maven Sonar scanner will still run with an empty token and fail the pipeline instead of being skipped as required.

This job can still run when `SONAR_TOKEN` is empty because the condition only checks Sonar variables. In repositories or pull requests where the variable is present but the secret is missing/unavailable, the Sonar scan action fails rather than being skipped, violating the stated non-failing behavior for absent Sonar credentials.

This condition does not include the `SONAR_TOKEN` secret, so the blockchain Sonar scan runs and fails whenever project variables are set but the token is absent or unavailable. The workflow needs to skip this scan when the token is missing to keep CI from failing on missing Sonar credentials.

The Sonar enablement check only verifies SONAR_TOKEN, but the workflow defaults to SonarCloud and omits sonar.organization when SONAR_ORGANIZATION is unset. With a token configured but the required project variables missing, this job will attempt analysis and fail instead of being skipped as the CI acceptance criteria describe.

The Sonar enablement check only verifies SONAR_TOKEN, but the workflow defaults to SonarCloud and omits sonar.organization when SONAR_ORGANIZATION is unset. With a token configured but the required project variables missing, this job will attempt analysis and fail instead of being skipped as the CI acceptance criteria describe.

The Sonar enablement check only verifies SONAR_TOKEN, but the workflow defaults to SonarCloud and omits sonar.organization when SONAR_ORGANIZATION is unset. With a token configured but the required project variables missing, this job will attempt analysis and fail instead of being skipped as the CI acceptance criteria describe.

These values use the Sonar job results, but when SONAR_TOKEN is absent each Sonar job still finishes successfully because only its analysis steps are skipped. The Telegram summary will therefore show the Sonar phases as ✅ success even though analysis did not run, which makes the pipeline status notification misleading.
